generated_comment:
Suresh: Arre, isme sexist kya hai? Jo jahan better ho wahi sambhale, practical baat hai. Mard bahar ke kaam, finance, dealing dekh lete hain, aur ghar ki routine, khana-peena, bachchon ka system aurat zyada naturally achchhe se sambhal leti hai.
Wife: Haan, sab equal-equal bolne se ghar thodi chalta hai. Agar pati kabhi chai bana de ya bachche ko school chhod de toh achchi baat hai, but main responsibility toh ghar ki aurat par hi suit karti hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it naturalizes a gendered division of labor as merely practical, assigning finances/external work to men and domestic management to women. It also encodes internalized misogyny through the wife's agreement and celebrates minimal male contribution as exceptional help rather than shared responsibility.
original_frame: The Character Double Standard: Women with Male Connections are Loose, Men with Female Connections are Desirable
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A businessman in Ahmedabad. You are discussing marriage proposals for your son Karan who is 27. A proposal has come from a good family Ñ the girl is educated, employed, from a respectable background. But you found out through a mutual contact that the girl has a large mixed gender social circle and is known to go out with male friends in groups. You are sitting with your wife telling her what you found out and what you think it means for the kind of wife she will be and whether this is the kind of girl you want for your son and what you are going to tell the girl's family.
